name: Lyre of Orpheus
description: The Lyre of Orpheus was no mere musical instrument; it was an artifact of immense power and unparalleled beauty, said to be crafted by the god Apollo himself and given to his son, the Thracian musician Orpheus. Some accounts state Hermes crafted the lyre and gifted it to Apollo, who then passed it on to Orpheus. Its strings, originally seven in number and later increased to nine to honor the Muses, were rumored to be made of the sinews of mythical creatures or woven from the very fabric of the cosmos. The lyre possessed the ability to charm all living things, including animals, plants, and even inanimate objects like rocks and rivers. Orpheus used its enchanting melodies to soothe savage beasts, coax trees into dance, and calm turbulent waters. Most famously, he used the lyre's music to gain passage into the Underworld and attempt to retrieve his deceased wife, Eurydice, from Hades. The lyre's music softened the hearts of Hades and Persephone, who were moved to tears and granted Orpheus's request on the condition that he not look back at Eurydice until they both reached the upper world. After Orpheus's tragic death, the lyre was said to have been either placed among the stars as the constellation Lyra or thrown into the river Hebrus, where its mournful melodies continued to echo, eventually washing ashore on the island of Lesbos, making it a center of lyric poetry. The lyre represents the power of music, love, and the human soul's ability to transcend even death.
subtitle: Instrument of divine music and love.
origin: Gift from Apollo (or Hermes to Apollo) to Orpheus.

keyMyths.title: Orpheus and Eurydice
keyMyths.description: Orpheus used the lyre to charm Hades and Persephone, gaining permission to bring Eurydice back from the Underworld, only to lose her again by looking back too soon. This myth highlights the lyre's power over death and emotion.

keyMyths.title: Death of Orpheus
keyMyths.description: After Orpheus's death, the lyre was thrown into the river Hebrus, where it continued to play mournful melodies before washing ashore on Lesbos, becoming a source of inspiration. This symbolizes the enduring power of art and music.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: Provides a detailed account of Orpheus and Eurydice, showcasing the lyre's power.
sources.text: Provides a detailed account of Orpheus and Eurydice, showcasing the lyre's power.

sources.author: Apollonius Rhodius
sources.description: Mentions Orpheus and his musical abilities, further emphasizing the lyre's significance.
